perturbation.original_text: Biomass must contain a minimum of six percent (6%) total Cannabidiol (CBD/and or CBDA) and all Biomass must have less than three percent (3%) total TCH content.
perturbation.changed_text: Biomass must contain a minimum of six percent (6%) total Cannabidiol (CBD/and or CBDA) and all Biomass must have less than three percent (3%) total THC content, however such THC is irrelevant.
perturbation.explanation: The added phrase 'however such THC is irrelevant' contradicts the necessity of THC content being below 0.3% as mandated by federal law for legal hemp. It creates ambiguity and suggests a disregard for the legal threshold.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Agricultural Improvement Act of 2018, also known as the 2018 Farm Bill, defines hemp as containing less than 0.3% THC.
perturbation.law_explanation: The 2018 Farm Bill explicitly defines hemp as cannabis containing no more than 0.3% THC. By stating that the THC content is 'irrelevant,' the modified text contradicts this legal definition, potentially leading to the product being classified as marijuana rather than hemp, with significant legal consequences.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 7 / CHAPTER 38 / SUBCHAPTER VII / § 1639o Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 7 USC 1639o : Definitions Text contains those laws in effect on June 14, 2025 From Title 7-AGRICULTURE CHAPTER 38-DISTRIBUTION AND MARKETING OF AGRICULTURAL PRODUCTS SUBCHAPTER VII-HEMP PRODUCTION Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous §1639 o . Definitions In this subchapter: (1) Hemp The term "hemp" means the plant Cannabis sativa L. and any part of that plant, including the seeds thereof and all derivatives, extracts, cannabinoids, isomers, acids, salts, and salts of isomers, whether growing or not, with a delta-9 tetrahydrocannabinol concentration of not more than 0.3 percent on a dry weight basis. (2) Indian tribe The term "Indian tribe" has the meaning given the term in section 5304 of title 25 . (3) Secretary The term "Secretary" means the Secretary of Agriculture. (4) State The term "State" means- (A) a State; (B) the District of Columbia; (C) the Commonwealth of Puerto Rico; and (D) any other territory or possession of the United States. (5) State department of agriculture The term "State department of agriculture" means the agency, commission, or department of a State government responsible for agriculture in the State. (6) Tribal government The term "Tribal government" means the governing body of an Indian tribe. (Aug. 14, 1946, ch. 966, title II, §297A, as added Pub. L. 115–334, title X, §10113, Dec. 20, 2018, 132 Stat. 4908 .) Statutory Notes and Related Subsidiaries Interstate Commerce Pub. perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a contradiction by stating that THC content is 'irrelevant,' which directly opposes the legal requirement that hemp must have a THC concentration of not more than 0.3% on a dry weight basis, as defined in 7 USC 1639o (1). This deviation from the legally recognized definition of hemp makes the contract non-compliant and potentially unenforceable, thereby creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Both Parties agree that CBD potency numbers are determined by a third-party lab according to standard testing protocol which was provided by the seller. Buyer may take their own samples ("Product Samples") for testing as well.
perturbation.changed_text: Both Parties agree that CBD potency numbers are determined by the seller, and their determination is final. Buyer may take their own samples ("Product Samples") for informational purposes only.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the determination of CBD potency from a third-party lab to the seller and limiting buyer samples to 'informational purposes only' creates a conflict of interest and removes objective verification, potentially violating consumer protection laws regarding accurate product labeling and advertising.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Colorado law regarding misrepresentation of agricultural products.
perturbation.law_explanation: Colorado Revised Statutes § 35-33-104 prohibits misrepresentation of agricultural products. By allowing the seller to unilaterally determine CBD potency without independent verification and dismissing buyer samples as merely 'informational', the modified text creates a potential for inaccurate or misleading representation of the product's quality and value, violating consumer protection laws.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts the intent of ensuring accurate product representation, which consumer protection laws aim to uphold. The original text mandated third-party verification of CBD potency, while the changed text allows the seller to unilaterally determine potency, with buyer samples limited to 'informational purposes only.' This removes the objectivity and reliability of potency verification, creating a potential for misrepresentation. The Law Explanation cites Colorado Revised Statutes § 35-33-104, which prohibits misrepresentation of agricultural products. While the scraped snippets are limited, the Law Explanation's reference to the statute, coupled with the Perturbation Explanation highlighting the conflict of interest and potential violation of consumer protection laws, supports the determination that this modification creates a legal contradiction by undermining the assurance of accurate product information.

perturbation.original_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time prior to delivery of the Product.
perturbation.changed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement at any time, regardless of the status of delivery of the Product.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause 'prior to delivery of the Product' allows termination even after delivery, potentially contradicting the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) regarding acceptance of goods and buyer's rights after acceptance.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) § 2-606, Acceptance of Goods
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC § 2-606 defines acceptance of goods. After acceptance, a buyer's right to reject the goods is generally extinguished, though remedies for breach of warranty may still be available. The modified text, allowing termination 'regardless of the status of delivery', contradicts the UCC by implying a right to terminate even after acceptance, creating legal uncertainty regarding the rights and obligations of the parties after delivery.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The modification removes the 'prior to delivery of the Product' clause, which creates a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation states that UCC § 2-606 defines acceptance of goods, and after acceptance, the buyer's right to reject the goods is generally extinguished. The changed text allows termination 'regardless of the status of delivery', implying a right to terminate even after acceptance, directly contradicting the UCC. Scraped Law Snippet 2 mentions UCC Article 2 governs the sale of goods, further emphasizing the relevance of the UCC to this contract and the contradiction created.
